data.title: Meituan: Capturing Brazil's Food Delivery Goldmine With Keeta
data.description:
At the recent “China-Brazil Business Seminar”, Meituan (OTCPK:MPNGF) announced that it plans to launch its overseas delivery platform, Keeta, in Brazil and pledges $1bn in investment in the next five years. This will be Keeta’s third ex-China destination following success in Saudi Arabia and Hong Kong.

Although we suspected that Keeta would pick an international destination that is closer to China given MT’s brand equity in the region and amongst Chinese users who have migrated to developed region such as Singapore, MT’s decision to expand into Brazil underscores its ability to execute on non-Asia regions on the back of its success in the GCC.

We reiterate our bullish view on Meituan. Our thesis on MT is that the company could be one of the few Chinese internet companies that has the potential to expand its footprint beyond China to offset China’s macro deceleration and market maturity by replicating its competitive moat in other emerging markets. Meituan has proven its overseas execution in the GCC, particularly in Saudi Arabia, where it is expanding beyond food delivery (see: Meituan: Riding On Keeta To Drive Overseas Growth) and in Hong Kong where it has successfully taken share from global peers (see: Meituan: On-Demand Momentum, Eyeing Lucrative Overseas Growth And Navigating AI Investments).

Although there were some concerns of rising competition in China on the back of JD’s entry into food delivery, we believe this competitive dynamic is manageable and that MT is likely to retain its leadership in food delivery, while JD could gain incremental share from a subscale player such as Alibaba’s Eleme.

We believe that MT can continue to replicate its success in Brazil, similar to how it captured a significant market share in the GCC and Hong Kong. On the back of an easing in the Sino-US tension, we believe that the stock is attractively priced at 12.8x 2026E earnings and should remain a long-term holding for any investor looking to gain a “China+” (China plus global growth) exposure.

A large and under-tapped market

The food delivery market in Brazil is projected to grow at a 15% CAGR in the next 8 years to reach $4.5bn by 2033, one of the fastest-growing markets in the region due to low food delivery penetration and rising merchant and consumer digitization.

The market is highly concentrated, with iFood dominating with a 70%-80% market share after Uber folded the market in 2022. Another major player is Rappi, which holds around 7% of the market share.

The heavy concentration by one player indicates potential for disruption, in our view. If we were to look at China, MT’s home market, JD’s recent entry into food delivery illustrates the arbitrage opportunity that a new entrant could capitalize on. Notably, gig workers in China are uninsured and lack benefits. JD’s proposition of full-time employee benefits for full-time riders, low merchant commissions, and the focus on licensed merchants allowed JD to capture some market share in the beginning, as it was widely viewed as socially responsible for both employees and consumers.

MT could leverage a similar tactic to be competitive in Brazil. We note that a typical commission rate for restaurants charged by iFoods is around 25%, with an average food delivery time of around 30-40 minutes. MT could offer lower commission to merchants to drive supply-side economics while leveraging its big data analytics to lower delivery time and offer a superior user experience to drive the demand-side economics.

Keeta’s differentiation

MT has announced that it is committing $1bn in the next five years to expand in Brazil. Much of the investment will be devoted to subsidies for both merchants and consumers, tech integration, and labor expansion, as MT has planned to hire 50k riders in the next 12 months.

Looking at the case studies from the GCC and HK, MT’s ability to expand market share in a short period of time was largely due to aggressive subsidies, localization of payment options and ensuring a consistent experience.

Similar tactics could be deployed in several areas of Brazil. On the merchant side, lower commission as we have mentioned, could drive supply-side scale. Assuming MT lowers merchant commission to 10-20% compared with iFood’s 25%, a typical merchant could save around $500/month which could be significant for smaller-scale merchants.

On the consumer side, MT has offered aggressive coupons and promotions to both GCC and HK-based consumers and the promotions for the Brazilian consumer should not be any less attractive, in our view.

Finally, rider bonus on timely delivery and delivery volume per day could drive some rider churn from iFoods to join MT as gig workers look to maximize earnings.

When we look at the geographic priority of focusing on Sao Paulo’s Villa Olimpia tech hub, this is very similar to that of rollout in Hong Kong where MT initially focused on a controlled area in Mongkok and Tai Kok Tsui to ensure execution consistency and testing before nationwide rollout.

Incremental revenue impact

We are bullish on MT’s entry into Brazil and believe that MT could take a meaningful share, translating to long-term upside surprises to the current consensus.

As we noted above, Brazil’s food delivery market is estimated to be $1.29bn with an expected 15% CAGR. Assuming Meituan captures 30% of the market, this would translate to $512mn in GTV share.

Assume that MT charges a blended 15% commission on food delivery and marketing services; this would translate to $77mn in revenue. Converting to Hong Kong dollars, we estimate that Keeta Brazil alone could have a 30bps revenue impact on MT.

Keep in mind that Brazil, along with GCC and Hong Kong are just one of many ex-China locations that MT can expand on and we believe that overtime ex-China revenue contribution could reach 10% of MT’s total shares in the next five-10 years, which is why we remain bullish on MT and continue to be positive on its ex-China execution as it looks to grow beyond China.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VIXY: The Fear Has Subsided (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

This year has been marked by a massive spike in volatility, move which tanked equities but also produced a rise in the price of long volatility products. We last covered one of these instruments in the beginning of April 2025, when we rated the ProShares VIX Short-Term Futures ETF (BATS:VIXY) a 'Strong Sell'. We usually do not attach 'Strong Buy' or 'Strong Sell' ratings unless it is a very high conviction trade. And selling VIXY was one:

The name is down -32% since our rating, retracing most of its move up. We find this instrument to be a very interesting one, falling in the short trades category, rather than a buy and hold. Given the move lower in volatility we believe the above trade is done, with investors being well-served in covering any shorts here and moving to 'Hold' (i.e. neutral). We are going to highlight in this article the mechanics of the risk factors involved, and why we believe the move lower in VIXY is now exhausted.

The VIX is back under 20

The 'fear gauge' or VIX, is now back under 20 after spiking as high as 50:

Please note how 'clean' the above chart is. The VIX is usually hugging the 15 handle in a normalized environment, with all the spikes higher normalizing over time. This makes sense from a financial structuring standpoint because VIX simply represents the implied volatility of options out to 30-days, thus it needs to be constantly bid to stay high. If investors suddenly are not fearful anymore, VIX naturally comes down as puts are not bought anymore.

With VIX now at 18, things are back to a relative 'normal', thus the next 'surprise' move in VIX is actually for the upside. If things stay as they are the volatility index will stay range bound between 15 and 20, thus not offering much upside in being short VIXY.

The futures curve is back in contango

Kindly keep in mind though that VIXY is a play on VIX futures, rather than the index itself:

The S&P 500® VIX Short-Term Futures Index utilizes prices of the next two near-term VIX® futures contracts to replicate a position that rolls the nearest month VIX futures to the next month on a daily basis in equal fractional amounts. This results in a constant one-month rolling long position in first and second month VIX futures contracts.

If we take a look at the current fund composition on its website, we get the following:

Most of the name is now invested in June 2025 futures, while the rest is in the May contracts.

It is important to remember that the fund invests in futures because the shape of the futures curve matters as well. Currently we are back in contango:

Contango is when the longer dated VIX futures have a higher value than the front month one. You can notice the upward sloping blue curve (which is the VIX futures curve). In a normalized environment the VIX futures curve is in contango, hence less of an incentive to short VIXY.

When a significant risk-off event occurs, the curve shifts to backwardation because everybody rushes to buy short dated puts for portfolio protection. Steep backwardation is a green flag to short VIXY. Not anymore.

While the market still has a small level of fear (the blue curve is above spot VIX), the mechanics now are tilted to another bout of risk-off rather than further tightening. Please keep in mind that risk-off equates VIXY higher, thus you would want to be long.

Short-term trading instrument

We cannot say this enough, but VIXY is a short term trading instrument. This is not a buy and hold, and positioning has to have clear entry and exit points for trades in VIXY to work. You want to short VIXY when fear prevails and the VIX spikes, and you want to move to neutral when the market normalizes. Occasionally, if one believes there is too much complacency you can also go long, but we do not like that trade very much because of the long-term roll effect and the fact that the fund tends to lose significant value over long periods of time:

The name is down -86% in the past three years, but has massive swings in between. This is not a buy and hold instrument, or a sell and hold instrument for that matter. VIXY is an ETF to use for short-term trading patterns only (in our view).

With our trade thesis now materialized (we were pleading for a normalization in our prior article) we are upgrading the rating here to Hold (i.e. neutral).

Conclusion

VIXY is an exchange traded fund. The name represents a long position in VIX futures, and spikes in price when the market experiences risk-off events. We assigned the fund a 'Strong Sell' rating as fear peaked in April, and the name is down -32% since. With the VIX index now under 20 and the futures curve in contango, we are upgrading the fund to 'Hold' given the lack of further upside in our original trade idea.

data.title: Grab: Two Major Catalysts Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

Grab (NASDAQ:GRAB) stock has had a volatile couple of months. Shares fell 40% from their February highs due to mixed Q4 earnings and Trump's Liberation Day. However, as weeks go by and as trade deals begin to form, Grab (along with the broader markets) recouped all its initial losses, rallying by about 50% from its April lows.

While tariff uncertainties may keep the stock zigzagging for the next few months, I believe Grab is more recession-resistant and tariff-resistant than many people think, and thus, the company should maintain strong fundamentals in the face of an economic storm — management confirmed this during their recent Q1 earnings update.

As you know, the leading superapp in Southeast Asia has gained a lot of buzz lately, especially among the retail investing community. In addition, a barrage of speculative news had investors on the edge of their seats.

Having said that, I believe Grab stock is still undervalued at $5 a share. While I believe Grab stock is well on its way to new 52-week highs, there are two major catalysts that could shorten this path.

Growth: Watch Out For Lending Acceleration

Looking at its Q1 results, Grab generated a record Revenue of $773M, up 18% YoY, with growth accelerating 1pp QoQ. This was driven by growth across all segments. This also beat analyst estimates by $10M.

Record Revenue was also driven by record Monthly Transacting Users (MTUs). In Q1, MTUs were 44.5M, up 16% YoY. Despite the seasonal impacts from the Lunar New Year and Ramadan fasting period, Grab added 0.6M New MTUs QoQ. This speaks volumes about Grab's incredible value proposition as the number one superapp in all of Southeast Asia.

Importantly, despite being the leading superapp in the region, Grab's user penetration is still low, with MTUs representing only 6% of the Southeast Asian population. Additionally, Daily Transacting Users only make up 16% of its MTU base, pointing to opportunities to improve engagement and monetization rates. Furthermore, Grab estimated that there are 119M Annual Transacting Users as of Q1, highlighting the company's potential to grow its MTU base multiple times from here, just by reengaging with "old" customers.

On-Demand MTUs grew 18% YoY, with On-Demand transactions up 21% YoY. These drove an 18% YoY growth in On-Demand GMV to $4.9B.

  • Deliveries GMV was $3.1B, up 16% YoY, driven by higher Deliveries transactions and Deliveries MTUs, which grew 17% YoY. Notably, Saver Deliveries now accounts for 35% of Deliveries transactions, up 9pp YoY, and Group Orders GMV grew over 300% YoY.
  • Mobility GMV was $1.8B, up 17% YoY, driven by higher Mobility transactions and Mobility MTUs, which grew 25% YoY and 20% YoY, respectively. A key highlight from the segment was Advance Booking GMV growing by over 200% YoY.

Looking at the table above, notice the slowdown in growth for both segments. This was due to "the Ramadan fasting period this year fully occurring within the first quarter, compared to 2024, where Ramadan occurred over both the first and second quarters". As such, we can expect a nice acceleration in On-Demand GMV growth in Q2 as YoY comps will be easier in Q2 — management confirmed this in their earnings update, citing a "strong demand recovery in April".

As a result of robust On-Demand GMV growth, Deliveries Revenue grew 19% YoY to $415M while Mobility Revenue grew 14% YoY to $282M.

Also worth mentioning is that Deliveries Revenue growth accelerated 6pp QoQ, primarily due to lower Deliveries Incentives of $350M, as compared to $363M in Q4, as well as higher Advertising Revenue.

Grab's Advertising business continues its upward trajectory, with Monthly Active Advertisers up 49% YoY and average spend from these advertisers up 30% YoY. While Advertising Revenue as a % of Deliveries GMV was flat QoQ (likely due to a seasonally softer advertising demand in Q1), the metric improved 40bps YoY to 1.7% in Q1, signifying improved ad take rates.

I can attest to this as I have noticed plenty of advertising activity, from the app's home page to car wraps and even the app's delivery tracking/navigation system (GrabMaps). Excitingly, Grab's advertising business is still lowly penetrated, indicating a long growth runway ahead. For instance, self-serve Monthly Active Advertisers were 191K as of Q1, which is only a 3% penetration rate against its merchant/partner ecosystem of about 6M.

As the advertising business continues to grow, it should contribute to high-margin Revenue growth, boosting the company's overall profits in the process.

Financial Services Revenue was $75M, up 36% YoY, driven by the lending and insurance units. Interestingly, Financial Services Revenue was up only $1M QoQ — this also happened in the same quarter last year, so I reckon Q1 to be a seasonally slow quarter for the fintech business. As such, we can expect an acceleration in Financial Services Revenue throughout the remainder of the year.

Nevertheless, the fintech business continues to display strong growth in Q1:

  • Loan Portfolio was $566M, up 56% YoY.
  • Loans Disbursed were $630M, up 30% YoY.
  • Digibank Deposits were $1.4B, up 199% YoY.

All things considered, management reiterated their full-year Revenue guidance of $3.33B to $3.40B, implying a 19% to 22% YoY growth. This also means that we can expect growth rates to accelerate over Q1's Revenue growth of 18%. Here are some of the initiatives that should drive growth in the short-to-medium term:

  • New products launched during the GrabX product event, including GrabFood For One (food delivery for solo diners), Shared Saver (group order with people nearby), and Dine Out Discovery (enhanced restaurant discovery and booking feature).
  • The launch of GrabCab in Singapore, a new street-hailing service fully operated by Grab.
  • The rollout of autonomous vehicles in Southeast Asia, following its new partnerships with four autonomous technology companies: Autonomous A2Z, Motional, WeRide, and Zelos.

While these are exciting projects for Grab, I think there's one area of Grab's business that could potentially take off: the fintech lending business.

It's important to note that the lending business is currently concentrated on Grab's driver-partners. However, this part of the business seems to be plateauing.

To fertilize its next stage of growth, management intends to ramp up consumer lending through its Digibanks (GXS Bank, GX Bank, and Superbank). By the same token, management plans to extend the lending business to more merchants in the future.

So in 2025, an increasing proportion of the lending will be to consumers, but we'll continue to deepen and enhance the relationship we have with lending to partners. I would say our penetration of drivers is the highest. We're still improving the models there and working out ways to facilitate smaller quanta and different tenures to more drivers. But I think that is starting to plateau. And therefore, that will grow only at the rate at which the number of drivers and the GMV they support comes on to the marketplace.

In terms of merchants, we are much less penetrated. And as we develop more and more capabilities for merchants like enhancing our payments enhancing our dine discovery which is the second part of your question that allows us to see more of their GMV and allows us therefore to extend our credit models and extend more lending to them.

(COO Alex Hungate — Grab FY2025 Q1 Earnings Call, emphasis added)

In other words, Grab's lending exposure to consumers and merchants is still very low, and now that all three Digibanks have loan products in the marketplace, Grab is ready to expand its consumer and merchant lending businesses, which, I believe, should generate extreme Revenue growth for the segment since consumers and merchants are much larger markets — demographically and financially — than drivers.

The growth of the lending business should be supercharged by higher cross-sell rates across the Grab ecosystem. For one, over 60% of its user base are now using two or more Grab offerings. Additionally, the majority of new Financial Services users are existing users of Grab's On-Demand services.

In the future, management also plans to expand GrabUnlimited — a premium subscription service that offers users additional Deliveries and Mobility benefits — to Financial Services.

As Grab continues to drive cross-sales across its ecosystem and as more users adopt GrabUnlimited, the Grab ecosystem becomes even more sticky, with each user generating even higher sales for the company.

For context, Grab's Revenue Per MTU has been hovering around $17 over the last two years, implying a low monetization rate. That said, through the combination of new product launches, higher cross-selling rates, and the expansion of the lending business, I expect this metric to explode in the coming years, delivering solid Revenue growth in the process.

Profitability: Raised Guidance Signifies Strong Operating Leverage

Jumping to the bottom of the income statement, Q1 Net Income was $10M, improving from $(115)M a year ago. Net Margin as a % of Revenue improved 19pp YoY, from (18)% to 1%. Higher Revenue, higher Net Finance Income, and lower Share-based Compensation drove this improvement.

EPS was $0.01, inline with estimates.

Looking at the profitability of each segment, the On-Demand segments saw sequential improvements while the Financial Services segment continued to burn cash as it is still in the early phases of growth:

  • Deliveries Adjusted EBITDA was $63M at a 2.0% Margin as a % of GMV, up 40bps YoY and 20bps QoQ. This was driven by increased contributions from Advertising and continued operating leverage in the segment. Though management is confident that they'll achieve their long-term Deliveries Margin of 4%+, they are currently prioritizing "absolute dollar expansion, EBITDA expansion versus a certain margin percentage", so expect margins to continue to fluctuate in the near term. The good news is that management is already seeing 4%+ Margins in certain countries, confirming the segment's potential to expand Margins to that 4% level.
  • Mobility Adjusted EBITDA was $159M at an 8.8% Margin as a % of GMV. While Margins dropped 10bps YoY, it improved 40bps QoQ. The YoY decline was driven by higher incentives to grow its driver supply base. On the other side, operating leverage within the segment led to a nice QoQ improvement in Margins.
  • Financial Services Adjusted EBITDA was $(30)M at a (41)% Margin as a % of Revenue. This was mainly due to "an increase in expected credit loss provisions as we continued to increase our loan disbursals". However, as the lending business explodes in the coming quarters, I expect this segment to turn a profit as it delivers serious efficiency gains from scaling.

In aggregate, Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA was $192M at a 3.8% Margin as a % of GMV, which improved 20bps YoY and 10bps QoQ.

Subtracting Regional Operating Costs of $86M, which improved from $91M last year, Grab produced a record Adjusted EBITDA of $106M in Q1, with Margins, both as a % of GMV and Revenue, improving significantly on a YoY basis.

As a result of strong growth and profitability performance in Q1, management is raising their profit guidance for the year, now expecting 2025 Adjusted EBITDA of $460M to $480M, up from $440M to $470M previously. In addition, management reiterated Financial Services Adjusted EBITDA breakeven by the second half of 2026 — I think they'll achieve this in the first half.

All things considered, Grab is displaying strong operating leverage, with the raised guidance as a vote of confidence. I expect this to continue in the ensuing years as Grab scales further.

Health: $5.9B of Net Cash But No Buybacks...

Q1 Adjusted Free Cash Flow was $(101)M, down from $(94)M last year and $69M from the previous quarter. This was due to higher working capital outflows typical of the first quarter, as well as higher capital expenditures.

On a TTM basis, however, Adjusted FCF is still positive, at $157M. This represents a TTM Adjusted FCF Margin of 5%, which improved 10pp YoY.

That said, one of the most interesting parts of the earnings report is that management did not repurchase any shares in Q1.

One potential reason for its zero buyback activity in Q1 is that Grab is gearing up for a major acquisition. The company has recently acquired Chope (a restaurant booking platform), Everise (a premium supermarket chain), and most recently, Validus (an all-in-one SME financing platform), so Grab is no stranger to the M&A world.

As you may or may not know, rumors are circulating that Grab is pursuing an acquisition of its biggest competitor in Indonesia, GoTo, the company behind GoJek and Tokopedia.

While this speculation has been ongoing over the last few years, let me tell you why I think this time is different:

  • This is the first quarter since the company authorized its $500M buyback program in Q4 of 2023, during which Grab did not buy back any shares. This is despite having $5.9B of Net Cash Liquidity, $274M of buyback capacity left, and the stock still down 70% from its highs.
  • In the past, GoTo has always denied merger talks with Grab. This time round, GoTo did not confirm or deny reports of a potential merger with Grab, only stating that "no agreement is in place between the Company and any party to enter into a transaction of the type that has been speculated on in the mass media".
  • Per Reuters, Grab has held discussions with several banks to act as funding partners. The acquisition value is rumored to be about $7B, and since Grab has only $5.9B of Net Cash, Grab would likely need to take out a loan to finance the acquisition. There were two separate sources with knowledge of this.

  • Grab has recently added Steven Tishman to the Board of Directors. Mr. Tishman has over 40 years of experience in investment banking and capital markets, specializing in... M&A deals. Coincidence? I don't think so.

Given these four developments over the past few months, I'd give it a high probability that a Grab-GoTo merger happens this year. From a fundamental standpoint, this deal would be highly synergistic, at least as it relates to the On-Demand segment (Gojek). This consolidation will ultimately cement Grab's leadership position in one of the most important markets in Southeast Asia: Indonesia.

But regardless of whether the deal goes through or not, I'm confident that Grab will still be a dominant force in the region for decades to come.

Valuation: Still Cheap

Quickly looking at the company's valuation, Grab is trading at an EV to Revenue of 5.3x, which is still attractive given the company's improving fundamentals, massive growth runway in Southeast Asia, and 30% Net Cash position.

As for me, I have a 12-month base-case price target of $7.15, which is relatively unchanged from my previous article. This represents an upside potential of about 41% from its current price of $5.06.

This assumes the following:

  • 2034 Deliveries GMV of $33.3B (11% CAGR) at a 4.5% Margin, inline with management's long-term margin guidance.
  • 2034 Mobility GMV of $24.1B (14% CAGR) at a 9.5% Margin, inline with management's long-term margin guidance.
  • 2034 Financial Services Revenue of $2.4B (25% CAGR) at a 35% Margin.
  • Altogether, this should produce Cash Flows (Adjusted EBITDA - CapEx) of $2.9B by 2034.
  • Perpetual Growth Rate of 2.5%.
  • Discount Rate of 10%.

I have also provided my bear and bull cases below.

As I've highlighted throughout this article, two major catalysts to watch out for are: 1) the acceleration of the Financial Services segment, and 2) the official news of a Grab-GoTo merger.

Risks

Recession

As you know, tariff talks are causing a lot of uncertainty across global markets. Unfavorable developments in trade deals may cause an economic slowdown, which may pressure Grab's growth as consumers pare back spending. However, I would argue that Grab is more recession-resistant than many would've thought, especially considering all the new, affordable services that just went live on the platform.

We are confident that the strong execution of our affordable and viral product- and tech-led strategies will increasingly position us as a counter-cyclical company, enabling us to remain resilient in an uncertain macroeconomic environment as we drive new user growth, improvements to usage frequency and user retention.

(CEO Anthony Tan — FY2025 Q1 Grab Earnings Call, emphasis added)

Growth Slowdown

While I believe Grab has a long growth runway ahead, one of my big concerns is that Grab's growth may slow down meaningfully if the company reduces incentives paid out to its consumers and its partners. There is no clear sign that this is happening just yet, since the company is still maintaining high incentives to drive growth, but this is one thing to be wary of in the future.

Thesis

As seen by its strong Q1 results, Grab's fundamentals continue to improve, with the company growing MTUs, Revenue, and Adjusted EBITDA to record highs.

Excitingly, Grab still has so much room for growth as it has a low penetration rate of just 6% and a low monetization rate of just $17 per MTU. This is why management is still investing heavily in growth, pumping high incentives and launching new products to maximize product adoption and user growth.

Meanwhile, at just $5 a share, the stock is still trading below intrinsic value, offering decent upside potential for long-term investors.

Moreover, investors can look forward to the eventual explosion of the lending business and the potential Grab-GoTo merger, both of which could serve as a major catalyst that'll launch the stock to new highs.

data.title: Maple Leaf Foods: Shares Are Cheap And They Have A Catalyst
data.description:
Even though I have lost over 83 pounds since late 2023, I still consider myself to be a foodie. Because of this, I love digging into food stocks, especially if they are trading on the cheap. One interesting the food business that's based not in the US, but in Canada instead, is Maple Leaf Foods (OTCPK:MLFNF). With a market capitalization as of this writing of US$2.32 billion, the company is of a decent size. Recent financial performance has been positive, with top line results and bottom line results showing meaningful improvements. Shares of the company are attractively priced on both an absolute basis and relative to similar firms. Furthermore, the company is continuing to work towards splitting itself off into two separate firms. At the end of the day, I believe that this combination of factors makes the company a very solid ‘buy’ candidate.

A necessary disclosure

For the purpose of this article, I would like to make clear that all references in it are in the form of Canadian dollars unless otherwise stated. Even though I am based out of the US and think in US dollar terms, and even though I would say that most of my readers are the same, the fact of the matter is that some of the investor presentation documents provided throughout this article show Canadian dollars. So for the purpose of avoiding any confusion, that will be the primary way in which I relay data for my readers.

Sampling Maple Leaf Foods

The management team at Maple Leaf Foods describes the company as a leading protein firm that owns multiple major brands in the markets in which it operates. These include, but are not limited to, Maple Leaf, Schneiders, Mina, Greenfield Natural Meat Co., Lightlife, and Field Roast. These brands focus on the preparation and sale of prepared meats, ready to cook and ready to serve meals, snack kits, value added fresh pork and poultry, and even plant protein products. Even though this is a Canadian business, most of its revenue comes from Canada, the US, and select parts of Asia. In fact, while Canada accounted for 74% of the company's revenue last year, the US, Japan, and China, all combined, accounted for another 20.1%.

In recent years, the enterprise has seen pretty consistent growth. From 2022 to 2024, revenue expanded from $4.74 billion to $4.90 billion. According to management, this rise in sales has been driven by both a rise in volume and a change in category mix. Poultry has been a bit of a weak spot for the company due to higher internalization of poultry supply into its prepared meats business, and because of what management described as the ‘repatriation of production’ to its London poultry facility. Plant protein sales have also been a bit weak because of weakness in the industry. And pork sales have been negatively affected by lower resale activity and an unfavorable change in product mix.

Even though the increase in revenue was not tremendous during this time, the improvement on the company's bottom line has been massive. From 2022 to 2024, the company went from a net loss of $311.9 million to a gain of $96.6 million. Some of this was because of some one time expenses earlier during this time. Specifically, I'm referring to the $190.9 million goodwill impairment that the company incurred in 2022. A reduction in restructuring and other related costs from $30.1 million to $19.9 million also helped. The biggest driver, however, has been a surge in the firm's gross profit margin.

In 2024, it totaled 15.93% of sales. That was nearly double the 8.95% that the company reported two years earlier. Most of that jump occurred between 2023 and 2024 and was, according to management, driven by improved pork market conditions, benefits from its London poultry facility, lower startup expenses and better project benefits involving its Bacon Centre of Excellence, a mark to market adjustment associated with biological assets, a rise in volume of prepared meats, and certain operating efficiencies that the company has been working on achieving.

With net income rising, cash flows have also gotten better. In 2022, operating cash flow for the company was only $49.3 million. Last year, it was almost 10 times that at $464.9 million. Over the same window of time, if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $157.3 million to $458.2 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company more than doubled from $272.9 million to $553.2 million.

Financial performance for the company has continued into the first quarter of 2025. Revenue for the business hit $1.24 billion. That represents an increase of 8.2% compared to the $1.15 billion reported one year earlier. Management said that this was because of a 7.1% increase in prepared foods that was driven by volume growth, higher pricing, foreign currency fluctuations, and changes in product mix. Poultry sales jumped 6% while pork sales skyrocketed 12%.

Overall, this is a positive development for the company. However, the picture on the bottom line was a bit mixed. Driven in part by a decline in gross profit margin from 19.7% to 17.5%, which management attributed to lower mark to market valuations for its biological assets, greater trade promotions, and unrealized losses on commodity futures contracts, net profits dropped slightly from $51.6 million to $49.6 million. Operating cash flow also dropped, falling from $87.3 million to $9.9 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $76.5 million to $114.7 million. In addition to this, EBITDA for the company grew from $116.4 million to $166.3 million.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management expects EBITDA to be around $634 million. If we assume that other profitability metrics will rise at the same rate, we would get adjusted operating cash flow for the year of $525 million. Using these estimates, as well as the historical results for the company for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are rather low multiples if you ask me. In the table below, I then stacked the company up against five similar firms. What I found was that, on a price to operating cash flow basis, Maple Leaf Foods ended up being cheaper than four of the five companies. And when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, I found that it was the cheapest of the group.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Maple Leaf Foods 7.1 8.7
J&J Snack Foods (JJSF) 14.1 12.5
Premium Brands Holdings Corporation (OTCPK:PRBZF) 13.3 11.3
Nomad Foods Limited (NOMD) 6.9 8.9
Utz Brands (UTZ) 11.3 16.1
WK Kellogg (KLG) 17.3 12.4

There are some other things to take into consideration when evaluating Maple Leaf Foods. For starters, over the last few years now, management has worked hard to reduce leverage. In the first quarter of 2023, the company had net debt of $1.68 billion. By the end of the first quarter of this year, net debt had dropped only slightly to $1.55 billion. But because of the massive improvements that the company saw from a profitability perspective, the overall net leverage ratio for the company was more than halved from 6 to 2.6.

Another thing to keep in mind is that, later this year, the business will be splitting up into two separate firms. The current company, Maple Leaf Foods will serve as a protein centric consumer packaged goods company that will get around 73% of its revenue from the sale of prepared foods. The rest of its revenue will come from its value-added poultry products. The other, separate publicly traded company will be known as Canada Packers and will include what is currently the pork side of the company.

The fact of the matter is that the pork industry has been incredibly volatile the last several years. In the image below, you can see the cost of hog production compared to the pork cutout from January of 2014 through the first quarter of this year. Higher feed costs caused in large part by drought conditions that limited alfalfa production, labor shortages, processing capacity reductions at major firms in the space, and other factors, caused this volatility. As the image shows, however, there has been stabilization in this market recently. Even though the tariff situation is less severe than it was even a few weeks ago, there are still worries about the impact that could come about from all of that mess. So I wouldn't say that the industry is completely out of the woods just yet.

If everything goes according to plan, the separation of these two companies will occur sometime in the second half of this year. There is going to be a shareholders’ meeting on June 11th during which investors will have the right to vote on the spin off. In the event that it does proceed forward, current shareholders of Maple Leaf Foods will retain a 19.9% stake in Canada Packers. For context, in the image below, you can see what the two separate companies should look like. This is based on trailing 12 month data as of the end of the most recent quarter. Management is hopeful that continued market normalization for the pork industry will lead to a bump up in annual EBITDA of between $40 million and $50 million for Canada Packers. But even if this comes to fruition, most of the value will be retained under the Maple Leaf Foods umbrella. We will obviously have to wait and see how the market values these two standalone businesses. But the hope for management is that this transaction will allow each side of the current operation to focus its efforts more optimally for the purpose of achieving greater shareholder value.

Takeaway

Without even factoring in the splitting up of the company, I would say that Maple Leaf Foods is attractively priced at this time. Admittedly, the company has dealt with a lot of volatility in recent years, but the most recent figures have been encouraging. Shares are cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to similar companies. And to the extent that any additional value is created from the spin-off that is in the works, it should only add to the bullish picture. Add all of this together, and I have no problem rating Maple Leaf Foods a solid ‘buy’ at this time.

Market Review

Equity markets began the new year with a rally on enthusiasm for an economy that would remain strong and a forecast for moderating inflation, lower interest rates, an extension of lower tax rates, and lower regulation. But rising concerns about a slowdown in earnings from large capitalization Technology companies, geopolitical tensions, and the President's announcement that tariffs would be detailed in early April pushed equities sharply lower to end the first quarter. Investors shifted to safety and U.S. Treasury yields fell.Small capitalization stocks as measured by the Russell 2000 declined nearly 11% in the first quarter and were down roughly 20% from their post-election highs in November 2024. We expect our investment approach to outperform in periods of heightened uncertainty, and we can report that our Micro, SMid and Mid Cap Growth strategies did indeed outperform in the first quarter. Our Small Cap Growth strategy held several stocks that experienced company-specific issues which led to modest underperformance relative to its benchmark in the first quarter. Performance for each of our primary strategies may be found in the below table, and detailed attribution commentary is available on the pages that follow.

Firm Update

In March, Conestoga announced succession plans for its flagship Small Cap Growth strategy. At the end of 2025, Derek Johnston, Partner/Portfolio Manager, will succeed Joe Monahan as Co-Portfolio Manager for Conestoga's Small Cap Growth strategy. Joe Monahan will continue to serve as Co-Portfolio Manager for Conestoga's Micro Cap Growth strategy and as an analyst supporting the firm's strategies until his retirement in late 2026.

Derek joined Conestoga in 2015, was named Co-Portfolio Manager for the SMid Cap Growth strategy in 2016 and became a partner in 2018. Derek has played a key role in growing the firm's SMid Cap Growth total assets from less than $100 million to nearly $2 billion. Along with his promotion to Co-Portfolio Manager for the Small Cap Growth strategy, Derek is also being elevated to a Managing Partner of Conestoga Capital Advisors, LLC. Derek's investment experience spans 29 years, which includes his experience prior to joining Conestoga at 300 North Capital, Engemann Asset Management and Banc of America Securities. He has a Bachelor of Science degree from Boston College and a Master of Business Administration degree from the Marshall School of Business at the University of Southern California. Derek earned the right to use the Chartered Financial Analyst ® designation in 2003.

Joe Monahan joined Conestoga in 2008 and has served as Co-Portfolio Manager of the Small Cap Growth strategy and Director of Research since the beginning of 2014. Joe's contributions to the firm have been significant and he played a key role in growing the firm's assets under management from less than $1 billion to nearly $7.8 billion today.

David Neiderer, Partner/Portfolio Manager, will assume Joe's role as a Director of Research and will also be elevated to a Managing Partner of Conestoga Capital Advisors, LLC. David joined Conestoga in 2013 and became a partner in 2018. David will also continue to serve as Co-Portfolio Manager for the firm's Micro Cap Growth strategy. David earned his Bachelor of Science degree in Economics and Accounting from the University of Richmond and a Master of Business Administration from The Wharton School at the University of Pennsylvania. He is a CFA Charterholder® and also a Certified Public Accountant.

As of March 31, 2025, Conestoga's total assets were $7.8 billion. Assets within our four primary institutional investment strategies were:

Our Initial Thoughts on the Tariffs Announcements

What are the potential ramifications of higher tariff rates on the Conestoga portfolios? It is quite difficult at this juncture to handicap the various potential outcomes given the fluid environment. With the Russell 2000 Growth down over 25% from its December high, a recession may already be embedded in market expectations.

We believe our companies are well positioned to handle this challenge. In Trump's first administration, we also faced tariff issues (albeit not as dramatic), but our companies repositioned their supply chains to adjust to the new environment. Additionally, our companies have historically had strong pricing power and highly differentiated products or services which enables them to better pass along higher costs generated by the tariffs. In addition, our companies' strong balance sheets and cash flow position them well to take advantage of the economic uncertainty.

In fact, the Conestoga strategies outperformed by meaningful margins from March 24th (when President Trump announced he would unveil new tariffs on automotive imports) through the completion of this letter on April 15th . The market's decline during this period was exacerbated by the bolder-than-expected reciprocal tariff announcement on April 2nd. From March 24th to April 15th, Conestoga's Small, SMid, Mid and Micro Cap Growth strategies all outperformed their benchmarks by several hundred basis points. Industries in which we typically do not invest were under particular pressure including semiconductors, retail, apparel, and commodity-based stocks. Furthermore, our Technology, Industrials, and Basic Materials sector holdings performed very well versus the benchmark constituents.

Over the past several months, we have focused our research efforts identifying the companies in the portfolios that we believe have a higher exposure to the tariffs given their supply chain and/or manufacturing footprint. Furthermore, we believe the domestic orientation of our portfolio positions us well. For example, the Small Cap Growth portfolio's revenue is predominantly sourced from U.S. ( ∼75% ) versus international ( ∼25% ) markets. While the tariffs are adversely impacting the equity markets and uncertainty may weigh on companies' near-term earnings, we also believe our small cap companies will benefit from the reshoring that is expected to take place because of the tariffs. Additionally, the Administration's focus on lower tax rates and less regulation should favor small cap companies. Our companies are well managed and focus on their long-term positioning and earnings power. We believe over the long-term our portfolio holdings will successfully manage this challenge through a combination of pricing power and more efficient operations. We will continue to monitor market developments and assess the impact of the tariffs on our portfolio companies.

Conestoga's Investment Philosophy and Approach

Philosophy

Our high quality conservative growth philosophy seeks to take advantage of the inefficient discovery process for micro, small, smid and mid capitalization companies and other investors' focus on near-term earnings. We employ our 'time horizon arbitrage' principles by identifying these higher quality companies that we believe are capable of growing through multiple business cycles.

Key Tenets of Our Style

  • High Quality Conservative Growth
    • We seek to invest in companies which we believe have sustainable earnings growth and strong balance sheets.
  • Patient, Long-Term Approach
    • We have a long-term investment horizon which typically results in a low turnover rate of 20-30%.
  • High Conviction
    • Range of portfolio holdings is expected to provide a balance between alpha generation and diversification.
  • Consistency of Returns with Low Volatility and Downside Capture
    • Consistently applied investment discipline has resulted in strong risk-adjusted returns over full market cycles.

data.title: Dividend Power Dogs: 3 Ideal Safe Buys For May
data.description:
Foreword

Only one on this Dividend Power list is too pricey, or reveals skinny dividends! Three of these thirty-four low-priced Dividend Power dogs are ready to buy because they also show “safer” dividends whose free cash-flow yield exceeds dividend yield. May finds: ZIM Integrated Shipping (ZIM), Mesabi Trust (MSB); Golden Ocean (GOGL), as the top three, living up to the dogcatcher ideal.

About Dividend Power

“How high are the earnings?

This strategy defines a universe of stocks based on earnings yield, with higher earnings yield considered better, because it signifies a low valuation.

How great is the yield?

We sort the universe of stocks by their dividend yield, picking the top 35.

What is the result?

The result is a portfolio that does a relatively good job weathering downturns, while still catching much upward motion during bull markets.

It is one of the simplest strategies that has also proven to be one of the most effective.”—YCharts

[See a summary of top ten break-even priced May Dividend Power Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and four “safer” choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 39.74% To 110.71% Net Gains From Top-Ten DiviPower Dogs By May 2026

Five of ten top DiviPower stocks (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this May 2025 yield-based forecast, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to May 2026 were:

Cool Co Ltd (CLCO) was projected to net $1,107.12 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta number was not available for CLCO.

Seanergy Maritime Holdings (SHIP) was projected to net $953.99 based on the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 21% more than the market as a whole.

Mach Natural Resources (MNR) was projected to net $843.45, based on dividends, a target price estimate from 4 analysts, less broker fees. A beta number was not available for MNR.

Advanced Flower Capital (AFCG) was projected to net $830.55 based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 5 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta number was not viable for AFCG.

MFA Financial Inc (MFA) was projected to net $629.58 based on the median of target prices estimated by 6 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 69% over the market as a whole.

ZIM Integrated Shipping was projected to net $572.70, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta number was not viable for ZIM.

Okeanis Eco Tankers (ECO) was projected to net $479.97 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 99% less than the market as a whole.

SFL Corp (SFL) was projected to net $467.06, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 53% less than the market as a whole.

Civitas Resources (CIVI) was projected to net $436.54, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 14 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 9% over the market as a whole.

SITE Centers (SITC) was projected to net $397.41, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 46% over the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 67.18% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten DiviPower stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 38% under the market.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

Top 35 May 2025 DiviPower Dogs By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top May DiviPower Stocks By Yield Ranged 13.77% To 74.11% Per YCharts

Top ten DiviPower stocks occupied five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

First place went to the lone industrials stock ZIM Integrated Shipping [1]. then. two energy representatives placed second and tenth, Mach Natural Resources [2], and BW LPG Ltd (BWLP) [9].

The first of five financials stalwarts, placed third, Triple Point Venture Growth BDC (TPVG) (3. The other four placed fifth to seventh and ninth, Advanced Flower Capital [5], OFS Capital (OFS) [6], MFA Capital [7], and Trinity Capital (TRIN) [10].

In fourth place stood the lone real estate member, SITE Centers [5],

Finally, in eighth place, the lone utilities member, Kenon Holdings Ltd (KEN) stood firm, to complete the May DiviPower list, by yield.

Top 35 May 2025 DiviPower Dogs By Analyst Target Prices

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top graph above shows relative strengths of the top ten May DiviPower list by dividend and price as of market close 5/13/2026.

This month 34 of the 35 DiviPower list, by yield, show projected annual dividends from $1k invested greater than their single share price.

Dividend Power rules! The lowest Power Dog cost per dollar of dividend was $1.35 from ZIM and the highest cost dividend dollar was exhibited by Bain Capital at $129.00 and Bain also failed to meet the dogcatcher Ideal.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by 34 of 35 on the May dividend power list.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Twenty of Thirty-Five DiviPower Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Eighteen Dividend Power stocks so infected are MNR, SITC, MFA, KEN, TRIN, AOMR, REFI, SFL, SCM, PFLT, SEVN, EFC, MFIC, NCDL, OBDC, CLCO GAIN, SHIP, RYN, and FPI. These are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, DOGE, Tariffs, Deals, and Uncertainty, too!

Ten Top Yield Dividend Power Dogs Show "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, all in the top ten are already ideally priced and their prices are all more than 65% below break-even.

Mr. Market is very effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts. Watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 24.36% To 102.7% Top Ten DiviPower Upsides To May 2026; (32). Four -0.36% To -86.02% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 8.21% Disadvantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 DiviPower Dogs As of April 4, 2026

Ten-top DiviPower dogs were culled by yield for this monthly update. All ten showed positive yield (dividend / price) results. YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten DiviPower dogs selected 5/13/25 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors: Industrial, Financial, Energy, Utilities, and Real Estate. Missing the top-ten cut were Consumer Discretionary, Consumer Staples, Communication Services, Health Care, Materials, and Technology.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend Power Dogs (34) To Deliver 42.15% Vs. (35) 36.64% May 13, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dividend Power list by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 15.03% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The seventh lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Mach Natural Resources showed top analyst-estimated gains of 84.35%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield Dividend Power dogs for May were: Advanced Flower Capital; TriplePoint Venture; OFS Capital; MFA Financial; BW LPG, with prices ranging from $5.77 to $11.46.

Five higher-priced Dividend Power dogs as of May 13 were: SITE Centers; Mach Natural Resources;Trinity Capital; ZIM Integrated Shipping; Kenon Holdings, with prices ranging from $12.42 to $32.66

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the post-election euphoria fades and the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements gain momentum into 2025 with the exception of the few AI stocks, watch all of the top ten Power dogs stay Fair-priced…

Afterword

Buy 3 Ideal ‘Safer' May Dogs of Dividend Power

April, 2025, 4 of the 35 Dividend Power Dogs were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 5/13 data showed four as fair-priced,

Dividend Power 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price “Break-Even” Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that all dividends from $1K invested in our top three ’safer' Dividend Power dogs conform to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) equal to the single share price of each stock; a so-called, “break-even price.”

No market correction is required to put all three share prices of these “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Dividend Power updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Three IDEAL ‘Safer” Dividend Power Dogs

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Dividend Power dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from YCharts; Yahoo Finance - Stock Market Live, Quotes, Business & Finance News; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open-source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: INDY: Look Through India's Border Tensions
data.description:
Geopolitical risks have risen in India following the launch of “Operation Sindoor”, a military response to last month’s terror attack in Pahalgam (considered the “deadliest attack on civilians in India” since 2008), which resulted in twenty-six civilian casualties. Pakistan has since retaliated against the Indian strikes, making this one of the more severe India-Pakistan conflicts in recent memory.

Despite the rapid escalation, this one has been very much unlike past crises in that both sides have also rapidly taken an off-ramp in the form of a (rather fragile) cross-border ceasefire.

This is largely thanks to where the geopolitical incentives lie this time around. On the one hand, Pakistan has the backing of China, but not the West, which appears more in favor of neutrality this time around. Add to that the push by the Trump administration, using trade (note India and the US are currently hashing out a trade deal) as a de-escalation tool, and you have all the ingredients for a timely off-ramp.

In any case, Indian markets have, rightly in my view, seen through the latest India-Pakistan conflict after a momentary dip. Prior years’ precedents (2019 Pulwama attack, 2016 Uri attack) suggest staying long equities is the way to go as well.

In sum, I would stay focused on India’s fundamentals, which screen attractively now that earnings numbers are tracking ahead of consensus this quarter. With foreign investor flows also rapidly reversing after a prolonged selloff (see INDY: Weathering The Storm With Indian Mega Caps), iShares India 50 ETF (INDY) stands out as a prime beneficiary.

INDY Overview – Focused on India's Mega Caps

Within the Indian ETF universe, INDY stands out from key comparables like the iShares MSCI India ETF (INDA) and Franklin Templeton's ultra-low-cost FTSE India ETF (FLIN) by concentrating on India’s fifty largest blue chips, as tracked by the flagship Nifty 50 index. There is one other US-listed Nifty 50 tracker, First Trust’s India Nifty 50 Equal Weight ETF (NFTY), though, unlike INDY’s capitalization weighting, NFTY is equal-weighted (see NFTY: India Enters The 'Panic Zone').

INDY isn’t quite as popular, though, with its $694m asset base paling in comparison to INDA’s $9.1bn and FLIN’s $2.2bn. Less size means less liquidity, as evidenced by its wider 0.12% bid/ask spread (vs. 0.02% for INDA and 0.08% for FLIN). Combined with this is a premium ~0.9% expense ratio (vs. ~0.6% for INDA and ~0.2% for FLIN), which makes this ETF not quite as fundamentally competitive as comparable Indian large-cap vehicles.

INDY Portfolio – Mind the Concentration

Post-rebalancing, INDY’s 50-stock portfolio is now more concentrated than before at the sector level. A lot of this is down to the resilience of its biggest sector exposure, Financials - an even larger overweight at 37.2%. By comparison, INDA runs its Financials allocation at almost eight points lower at 29.3%, while FLIN’s exposure is an even lower 28.6%. Other major sector allocations here include Information Technology (down to 11.5% amid tariff turbulence), Consumer Discretionary (up to 11.0%), and Energy (flat at 10.2%).

INDY’s single-stock allocation is also quite concentrated. In line with last quarter, the fund’s largest holdings remain India’s two major private banking groups, HDFC Bank (HDB) and ICICI Bank (IBN), albeit at an upsized 13.1% and 9.0%, respectively. Conglomerate Reliance Industries (RLNIY) is also up to 8.6% after a strong year, though IT services leader Infosys (INFY) has lost ground. In total, the top five contribute an outsized 40.4% (vs 28.4% for INDA and 25.7% for FLIN), so investors will want to be comfortable with a high level of concentration here.

Expect some changes, however, ahead of a major MSCI index “rejig” later this month. On the one hand, India will not only maintain its MSCI Emerging Markets weighting (second-largest at ~19.5%) but also net gain additional passive inflows from the move. On the other hand, INDY will see many of its larger holdings, from HDFC Bank to Infosys, downsized by MSCI in favor of smaller holdings like Cipla, Ultratech Cement, and Grasim. Net-net, I do expect concentration levels to ease as a result, though the impact shouldn’t be too material.

INDY Performance – Leading Through the Bear Market

India’s bull markets tend to be led by higher-growth smaller caps, but in bear markets, like the one we saw in recent months, mega-cap-focused INDY outperforms. Year-to-date, INDY is the pick of the large-cap trackers at +3.9% (vs +1.9% for INDA and +1.9% for FLIN); similarly, over the last one year, INDY’s total return stacks up nicely against both INDA and FLIN.

Zoom out, however, and the fund has lagged its ETF comparables, albeit with very respectable +14.7% and +7.5% annualized NAV returns over the last five and ten years, respectively. The key is that, because in the last decade, India has been in a raging bull market, the mega caps have relatively underperformed smaller caps. In a bear market, on the other hand, the inverse tends to hold true. In essence, owning INDY is less about maximizing upside than balancing this upside with some downside protection as well.

The other plus to owning INDY is that it also tends to trade at a lower forward P/E than other India trackers. And having de-rated quite a bit in recent months, largely thanks to heavy foreign investor selling, this Nifty 50 tracker now looks quite undemanding relative to its outlook for mid-teens % earnings growth and returns on equity.

Summing Up

Ongoing India-Pakistan border tensions represent a tail risk, but if history is any indication, staying long in Indian equities tends to pay off eventually. In the meantime, there’s a growing technical case to be long INDY, given mega-caps are prime beneficiaries of a resurgence in foreign investor flows. A fundamental one too, given India’s relatively low exposure to trade-related disruptions, and with earnings tracking ahead of expectations this reporting season. Net-net, INDY offers a compelling risk/reward here.

data.title: AMD: A Losing Bet On AI
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) recently reported its Q1 results. While the results were decent, as its revenues increased by 36% Y/Y, we see several risks that the company faces, which in our opinion are likely to undermine AMD’s growth opportunities. Although AMD’s stock price has slightly increased after our bearish call in March, we still believe that the company is uninvestable.

Why We Remain Bearish About AMD

One of the biggest issues that AMD currently faces is the implementation of stricter export controls by the United States, which are going to affect the company’s performance in Q2 and the rest of 2025. AMD’s Q2 guidance already reflects a $700 million revenue reduction as a result of new U.S. export restrictions on Instinct MI308 GPUs shipped to China. Overall, the management currently estimates a negative impact of $1.5 billion in 2025, which is a major issue in our opinion.

While AMD will likely try to offset the China-related losses by trying to grow sales in the United States and other markets, there’s a risk that such a plan might fail considering the recent weakening of the American economy. Also, China has been a significant early adopter of AMD’s AI accelerators. The loss of the Chinese market makes it harder for AMD to aggressively grow at a time when Nvidia’s (NVDA) dominance in the AI GPUs business remains unchallenged.

Another way to offset the China-related losses would be to launch the next generation of AI accelerators. That’s exactly what the company is planning by accelerating the launch of GPUs from the MI350 series this year. However, there’s no guarantee that an accelerated launch is going to greatly help the company in the end. So far, no major AI lab or a big large language model ("LLM") player has publicly committed to large-scale deployment of infrastructure based on the MI350 chips. This is why we believe that it’s too optimistic to believe that the GPUs from the MI350 series will save the day.

Another risk that AMD faces is the fading of the momentum in the client and gaming segments. Last quarter, AMD’s gaming revenue fell by 30% Y/Y due to weak demand for semi-custom products. Although the sales of Radeon graphics cards were recently strong, they are unlikely to offset the decline in console-related SoC sales later this year, especially given the maturing console cycles.

All of this makes us remain bearish about AMD and its future.

Risk To Our Bearish Thesis

Despite our bearish stance, it’s important to highlight AMD’s strengths to figure out if they would be able to undermine our thesis.

One major thing that AMD has going for it is the growth of the data center processor market, which is not stopping for now and could help AMD boost its overall sales in the future despite all the risks. While Nvidia dominates the market for now, AMD is currently establishing itself as a solid second option for AI accelerators.

Also, AMD has a diversified portfolio of products, which is another of its strengths. With its exposure to desktop, mobile, and could markets, AMD is diversified enough to weather the current macro volatility and potentially perform better than expected in the upcoming months.

The Real Value of AMD

To determine AMD’s intrinsic value, we created a valuation model. The valuation model was created when AMD was trading at $102.84 per share. We applied a perpetual growth rate of 3% in our model, consistent with the rate we typically use when valuing other semiconductor businesses. The forecast covers the upcoming five years, with inputs such as cash and long-term debt sourced from the latest earnings report.

The discount rate in the model is 10.46%. We calculated it by figuring out AMD’s cost of equity and cost of debt. To calculate the cost of debt, we mostly used its TTM numbers. For the cost of equity calculation, we used a risk-free rate of 4.38%, a beta of 1.87, and a market return rate of 7.77%. Later, we weighted AMD’s debt and equity to figure out its discount rate.

In the forecast table, we largely align our sales and EBIT projections with the broader market consensus for the next five years. We also assume a 15% tax rate in the model, consistent with the rate applied in our earlier valuations of semiconductor companies. The changes in net working capital in our model are expected to be 1% of sales, which is the rate that we also used when creating a valuation model for other semiconductor businesses. The rest of the assumptions in the model closely align with AMD’s historical performance.

Under those assumptions, our valuation model estimates AMD’s enterprise value to be $87 billion. By adding cash and cash equivalents to the enterprise value and subtracting the total debt, we can calculate AMD’s equity value, which in our case is $88.4 billion. By dividing the equity value by the number of outstanding shares, we arrive at AMD’s intrinsic value of $54.00 per share, which is about 47.49% below the current market price.

Final Thoughts

The loss of China-related revenues as a result of the stricter export restrictions and the overvaluation of the company’s shares makes us believe that AMD remains uninvestable at this point. It certainly has some strengths, but they are not enough to justify a long position in the company. That is why AMD remains a Sell for us.

data.title: Buckle Up For An Economic Deceleration
data.description:
Stocks defied gravity for a fourth session in a row, thanks to lower Treasury yields across the curve on the tailcoats of mixed economic data that gave investors hope for more interest rate cuts from the Fed. The reasoning changes from one day to the next, but this runaway train that pulled out of the station early last month feels like it is running on fumes. I have been early in my call that risk asset prices would top, but rising stock prices are about to run into deteriorating economic data, and I don’t think the Federal Reserve will be in a rush to react as soon as the Trump administration was in early April.

I found the chart below telling in terms of how much risk we now have in the market after a historic rise in prices. Taxes on imported goods, otherwise known as tariffs, rose from an effective rate of 2.3% to more than 24% on April 2, sending the major market indexes cascading in the days that followed. That led to a surge in volatility, correlation, and potential for dispersion as measured by option prices. Today, after several delays, rollbacks, and two agreements, the effective rate is approximately 15%. Yet the macro risks reflected in markets are less now than they were before April 2. I think the market is now greatly mispricing risk.

Investors were encouraged by the report that producer prices (PPI) declined last month by 0.5% to an annualized 2.4%, which was below the 2.5% expected and 3.4% we saw in March. The core rate, which excludes food and energy, increased to an annualized 3.1% from 2.5% the month before. As I said with respect to the Consumer Price Index (CPI), I think we are seeing a trough in prices, as tariffs are just starting to have an effect. Inflation is the most lagging of all indicators, which tells us more about where we have been than where we are going.

Retail sales rose just 0.1% in April, but that compares to an upwardly revised 1.7% increase in March, so the annualized rate held steady at a very strong 5.2%. It appears that consumers ramped up purchases of goods in advance of expected price increases, which started to wane some in April, as seven categories out of 13 in this report saw sales declines, led by import-related autos, department stores, apparel, and sporting goods. That freed up some spending for services, as bars and restaurants saw strong sales growth of 1.2%. Again, price increases have not taken a toll yet, but they are coming.

Walmart reported mixed results for the first quarter yesterday, falling a little short on sales with better-than-expected earnings, while same-store sales exceeded estimates with growth of 4.5%. CEO Doug McMillan indicated on the conference call that tariffs were leading to price increases in April and May, and that the “reset of costs” will continue through the year. For the country’s largest retailer, that flies in the face of those who have been advocating that tariffs are not paid by US businesses and consumers. They indisputably are, as evidenced by Walmart.

While I am forecasting an increase in the rate of inflation and deceleration in the rate of consumer spending growth, the proof will show up in the chart below. We are on the warning track when real (inflation-adjusted) personal spending, which includes goods (retail sales) and services, starts to approach zero. In March, the rate was a healthy 3.3%. For me, this is the tip of the spear of an oncoming downturn in the economy. My expectation is that the market will start to discount that downturn, if it happens, well in advance. That’s why I remain cautious over the summer months.

data.title: Meren: A Sustainable 20% FCF Yield And 11% Dividend
data.description:
Africa Oil Corporation (OTCPK:AOIFF) has announced it's changing its name to Meren Energy. The $900 million company recently announced very impressive 1Q 2025 results, as the company has continued to pay-off its balance sheet and drive strong shareholder returns after its merger with Prime Oil and Gas.

Africa Oil Corporation Results

Africa Oil Corporation had more than $50 million in net income from more than 37K barrels in entitlement production.

The company managed to generate a massive $122 million in FCF and YoY it's managed to reduce its net debt position from almost $286 million to almost $192 million. The company's substantial usage in its net debt despite its substantial new dividend of $100 million annualized shows its continued annual cash flow.

Africa Oil Corporation Dividends

The company's new dividend policy is $25 million per quarter or just under $0.04 / share.

The company has announced its latest dividend that will be paid in roughly 4 weeks to shareholders of record at the close of business on May 26. That more than $100 million in annual dividend is a double-digit dividend yield versus the company's present market cap of $900 million, making this one of the few companies with an affordable double-digit dividend.

Africa Oil Corporation Nigeria

The company's core operations are in Nigeria where the entirety of the company's production is located.

This is visible in the company's YoY decline as the company needs to focus on cleaning up its portfolio and generating long-term production. The company is continuing to explore potential assets here for example Akpo Far East that could have 144 million barrels of oil equivalent, which would help the wells here.

The company is continuing to focus on maintaining portfolio production and exploration which is key because it's the company's only producing assets driving all cash flow.

Africa Oil Corporation Namibia

In Namibia, the company is focused on the Venus Field, with a potential FID to be made in H1 2026.

Africa Oil and Gas has a net 4% ownership in the Venus Field through its ownership in Impact Oil & Gas. The new FPSO development plan here is expected to support up to 160K barrels / day of production, which would be more than 6K barrels / day attributable to Africa Oil and Gas. Respectable but not enough to replace its core production.

Still this is a key development for the late 2020s and additional drilling campaigns here are incredibly important. This is a multi-billion barrel field that could support hundreds of Ks of barrels / day in production for the company.

Africa Oil Corporation Guidance

The company's guidance for 2025 shows production coming in below its 1Q 2025 results as uncertainty remains.

The company is basing things on $75 / barrel Brent implying a 12% reduction versus the midpoint on the financial guidance. That would imply roughly $310 million of cash flow at the midpoint, which after $170 million in capital investment, would imply $140 million in FCF for the company. That's enough for the company to comfortably cover its dividend.

The company paying down its debt is key to reducing roughly $60 million in annual interest expenses which would be key to the company improving its FCF and shareholder returns. It would more than double the company's post dividend FCF.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices. The company is very profitable at current levels and it's continuing to improve its debt load and drive shareholder returns. We'd like to see more share buybacks at current prices, however, at $65 / Brent, with the potential to fall further, that'd hurt the company's FCF substantially.

Conclusion

Africa Oil Corporation is a great company with an impressive portfolio of assets. The company is worth almost $1 billion today and it's paying out a double-digit dividend yield. At the same time, the company is using its additional cash flow to rapidly improving its cash position and pay down its debt.

Given its higher bond cost we'd like to see it use cash to pay-down debt further helping reduce its $60 million in annual interest expenditures. The company partially did that with a further $80 million RBL balance reduction post quarter end. We have some concerns about the company's future long-term production, but with exciting exploration assets, we're confident in its ability to drive long-term shareholder returns.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
